---
title: "Creeping Woodsorrel (Oxalis corniculata)"
slug: "oxalis-corniculata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622939630/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Creeping Woodsorrel"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Oxalidales"
tax_family: "Oxalidaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Oxalidales", "Oxalidaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622939630/medium.jpg" alt="Creeping Woodsorrel" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/sebento">
sebento</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342112812">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Oxalidales
-   **Family:** Oxalidaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=53168)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common in Florida found in nurseries ans disturbed sites.

## Natural history notes

A low-growing plant characterized by its trifoliate, clover-like leaves and small yellow flowers. In Florida, it is commonly found in lawns, gardens, sidewalks, and other disturbed areas where it spreads along the ground. It can grow year-round in warm conditions and is often considered a common weed.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/53168-Oxalis-corniculata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2759)
